entries.name: Caregiver
entries.one_liner: A voice of practical compassion that names the difficulty before any reassurance and stays close to bodies, routines, and immediate next steps.

entries.name: Coach
entries.one_liner: A facilitative voice that builds capacity through questions and reflection, acknowledges complexity before offering direction, and creates space without abandoning the reader.

entries.name: Columnist
entries.one_liner: An opinionated, recurring perspective - the writer who has a recognizable stance, makes arguments in public, and is willing to be quoted on it.

entries.name: Direct Communicator
entries.one_liner: A plain, no-ceremony voice that states its purpose in the first sentence, does not build up to the point, and treats reader time as the primary resource to protect.

entries.name: Executive
entries.one_liner: A senior business leader's voice that leads with outcomes and decisions, names uncertainty explicitly, and uses "we" to signal shared accountability.

entries.name: Friendly Mentor
entries.one_liner: A warm, patient voice that assumes the reader is capable but new, explaining concepts by building from what they already know.

entries.name: Journalist
entries.one_liner: A reporting voice that attributes claims to sources and arranges facts in a sequence the reader can follow, resisting editorializing while still making the story work.

entries.name: Operator
entries.one_liner: An accountability-driven, hands-on voice that cares about what actually happens at 2am when something breaks - not the design, but the execution.

entries.name: Pastoral
entries.one_liner: A voice of care, scripture, and application - speaking from a shepherd's heart to a flock navigating real life with faith.

entries.name: Pragmatic Architect
entries.one_liner: A senior technical voice that leads with tradeoffs, names constraints explicitly, and treats every design decision as a bet with known odds.

entries.name: Product Thinker
entries.one_liner: A product manager's voice that leads with "why" before "what," centers user outcomes over implementation, and asks what job the reader is trying to do.

entries.name: Researcher
entries.one_liner: A disciplined voice that treats writing as the presentation of evidence, hedging where the data is thin and committing where it is strong.

entries.name: Senior Consultant
entries.one_liner: A polished advisory voice that diagnoses a situation against a named framework before recommending action, comfortable with hedged confidence.

entries.name: Storyteller
entries.one_liner: A voice that carries meaning through character, scene, and concrete moment, trusting the reader to extract the principle from the specifics.

entries.name: Technical Writer
entries.one_liner: A precise, reader-centered voice optimized for task completion - writes to the reader's goal, not the writer's knowledge, using plain language and imperative mood.

entries.name: Candid
entries.one_liner: Names the uncomfortable truth directly - not harsh, but unwilling to pretend the hard thing is not there.

entries.name: Celebratory
entries.one_liner: Marks genuine achievement by naming the specific thing, why it mattered, and inviting the reader to feel its weight - not hollow praise, not a list of everything at once.

entries.name: Confessional
entries.one_liner: The writer admits something about themselves - a specific failure, doubt, or learning, owned without performance.

entries.name: Confident
entries.one_liner: The affect of someone who has thought about this and is ready to say so, without hedging or padding the claim.

entries.name: Diplomatic
entries.one_liner: Careful, face-saving communication that is soft on people and firm on positions, especially across power differentials.

entries.name: Empathetic
entries.one_liner: Acknowledges the reader's specific experience before asking anything of them - earns the right to continue by naming the difficulty accurately.

entries.name: Encouraging
entries.one_liner: Speaks to capability and forward motion - not false praise, but genuine belief that the person can do the hard thing.

entries.name: Instructional
entries.one_liner: Patient, structured teaching that measures its own success by whether the reader can do the thing - not by how much it explains.

entries.name: Matter of Fact
entries.one_liner: States what is true without editorial coloring - neither cold nor warm, just accurate.

entries.name: Playful
entries.one_liner: Light, witty, and inviting - makes the pleasure of reading part of the point without sacrificing substance or becoming a performance.

entries.name: Resolute
entries.one_liner: The tone of having stopped deliberating - the decision is made and the writing is now about acting on it.

entries.name: Reverent
entries.one_liner: Approaches the subject with deliberate care and weightiness - the tone of someone in the presence of something that matters.

entries.name: Skeptical
entries.one_liner: Measured doubt that refuses to commit ahead of the evidence and asks what would actually change the picture.

entries.name: Urgent
entries.one_liner: Clarity under real pressure - the first sentence is the most important thing, and every word after it earns its place by not slowing the reader down.

entries.name: Warm
entries.one_liner: Communicates genuine care and personal regard for the reader - not flattery, but authentic human warmth.

entries.name: Chronological Narrative
entries.one_liner: Time order is the primary organizing principle - first this, then that, then what came after - with no thematic restructuring.

entries.name: Classical Argument
entries.one_liner: The Toulmin structure of claim, grounds, warrant, and rebuttal - making a position defensible through explicit reasoning.

entries.name: Comparison-Contrast
entries.one_liner: Places two or more options, ideas, or states side by side to illuminate the differences that matter.

entries.name: Decision Log
entries.one_liner: A real-time record of context, options considered, criteria used, and reasoning - capturing how a decision was reached, not justifying it after the fact.

entries.name: Definitional
entries.one_liner: Leads with a definition and elaborates through its facets, edge cases, and what-it-is-not - the definition is the load-bearing first move.

entries.name: Devotional Reflection
entries.one_liner: A slow, inward-turning movement that takes a scriptural or spiritual observation and follows it into personal and communal life.

entries.name: Dialectic
entries.one_liner: Thesis, antithesis, synthesis - states the position, states the strongest opposing position, and moves to a synthesis that takes both seriously.

entries.name: Diataxis Explanation
entries.one_liner: The Diataxis "Explanation" mode - oriented toward understanding rather than action, building a conceptual model for the reader.

entries.name: Executive Summary
entries.one_liner: Inverted-pyramid writing for decision-makers - recommendation first, supporting analysis second, structured so that stopping after paragraph one still leaves the reader equipped to act.

entries.name: Layered Disclosure
entries.one_liner: Progressively reveals depth - the first paragraph serves the casual reader completely, and each subsequent section adds detail for those who want it.

entries.name: Narrative Case Study
entries.one_liner: A story with a before, a turning point, and an after - using one specific real situation to make a general principle concrete and trustworthy.

entries.name: Problem-Solution
entries.one_liner: Frames the piece as a diagnosis followed by a remedy - establishes the pain before the cure.

entries.name: Procedural
entries.one_liner: Task-first writing that takes a reader from "I don't know how" to "I did it" by organizing every decision around user goals, not system features.

entries.name: Question and Answer
entries.one_liner: Sections are real reader questions in their natural phrasing, ordered by likelihood of being asked rather than by logical flow.

entries.name: Socratic Inquiry
entries.one_liner: Advances by asking questions the reader answers in their own head - the conclusions are inferences, not assertions.

entries.name: Architecture Decision Record
entries.one_liner: A short structured document that captures a significant architectural decision, its context, and its consequences.

entries.name: Blog Post (Long Form)
entries.one_liner: A substantial web article of 1,500-3,000 words - long enough to go deep, short enough to respect the reader's time.

entries.name: Changelog Entry
entries.one_liner: A structured release entry naming what was added, changed, fixed, or removed in a given version - grouped by impact class under a version heading.

entries.name: Cold Outreach
entries.one_liner: A brief first-contact message to a stranger that earns a reply by leading with their interest, not yours.

entries.name: Cover Letter
entries.one_liner: A letter accompanying an application that makes the case for one specific person for one specific role.

entries.name: Daily Standup
entries.one_liner: A brief daily status communication with three fixed sections - done, next, blockers. Surfaces information and flags what needs action. Not a progress report; a coordination tool.

entries.name: Design Document
entries.one_liner: An engineering document that explores a design space and justifies an approach before implementation.

entries.name: Devotional Entry
entries.one_liner: A short, spiritually-oriented piece designed for daily reading - typically anchored to a scripture passage and closing with reflection or prayer.

entries.name: Email
entries.one_liner: A business message designed for the inbox scan - subject line doubles as summary, body leads with action, and the reader never needs to re-read to know what is being asked.

entries.name: Eulogy
entries.one_liner: A tribute that honors a person through specific, concrete memory rather than abstract praise.

entries.name: FAQ
entries.one_liner: A list of anticipated questions with direct, self-contained answers, ordered by how often they are asked.

entries.name: How-To Guide
entries.one_liner: A task-focused guide that teaches a reader to accomplish something they do not yet know how to do.

entries.name: Incident Report
entries.one_liner: A customer-facing account of a service incident: what happened, the impact, and what is being done.

entries.name: Journal Entry
entries.one_liner: A private, dated reflection written for the writer, not for an audience.

entries.name: Landing Page
entries.one_liner: Web copy written to convert a visitor into a single action - sign up, buy, book, or subscribe.

entries.name: Meeting Notes
entries.one_liner: A structured capture of what was decided and what was assigned - not a transcript. Organized by outcome so someone who missed the meeting can act without asking follow-up questions.

entries.name: Obituary
entries.one_liner: A written death notice that records a life for the public - who the person was, what they did, and who survives them.

entries.name: One-Pager
entries.one_liner: A single-page document that makes one argument or presents one situation. The page constraint is the discipline - everything that does not fit does not belong.

entries.name: Op-Ed
entries.one_liner: A short argued opinion piece for a publication that advances one clear position on a timely issue.

entries.name: Personal Essay
entries.one_liner: A first-person reflective essay that uses a particular lived experience to reach a larger insight.

entries.name: Postmortem
entries.one_liner: A blameless retrospective of an incident: what happened, why, the impact, and the changes that keep it from happening again.

entries.name: Prayer
entries.one_liner: A written address to the divine - petition, thanksgiving, confession, or praise.

entries.name: Product Requirements Document
entries.one_liner: A structured document that defines what a product or feature should do, for whom, and why - without specifying how to build it.

entries.name: Press Release
entries.one_liner: A formal external announcement in news style: dateline, headline, a strong lead, a quote, and boilerplate.

entries.name: README
entries.one_liner: The front-door document for a software project - tells a first-time visitor what it is, why it exists, how to use it, and where to go next.

entries.name: Release Notes
entries.one_liner: A user-facing summary of what changed in a release and why it matters to the reader.

entries.name: RFC (Request for Comments)
entries.one_liner: A design proposal circulated to invite critique before a decision is made.

entries.name: Runbook
entries.one_liner: A step-by-step operational procedure for a recurring task or incident, written to be followed correctly under pressure.

entries.name: Slack Message
entries.one_liner: A short, async-first message designed for team channels - direct, scannable, and respectful of the reader's attention in a high-volume feed.

entries.name: Status Report
entries.one_liner: A periodic async update covering progress since the last report, what is next, and what is blocked - the working unit of distributed-team visibility.

entries.name: Support Reply
entries.one_liner: A written response to one customer's specific question or problem that resolves it and leaves them able to act.

entries.name: Technical Reference
entries.one_liner: A stable lookup document designed for repeated return visits. Optimized for scanning over reading. The reader arrives with a specific question; the format serves that question.

entries.name: Thank-You Note
entries.one_liner: A short, specific note of gratitude focused on one person and one thing they did.

entries.name: Toast
entries.one_liner: A short, warm tribute delivered aloud to celebrate a living person or a happy occasion.

entries.name: Tweet Thread
entries.one_liner: A sequence of numbered short posts (1/, 2/, 3/...) each under 280 characters, telling one connected story or making one connected argument across the chain.

entries.name: Whitepaper
entries.one_liner: A long-form authoritative document presenting a position, framework, or analysis - the format for setting position-of-record on a substantive topic.
